comment_text.sn12.18:4.1: The feeling is believed to be an inalienable property of the eternal Self who feels. Though eternalism is not explicitly mentioned, it is implied by the parallel construction of this discourse with the previous, except with feeling and the feeler rather than deed and the experiencer. In that discourse, the phrase “for one who has existed since the beginning” is part of the eternalist view, not the annihilationist, and the same is true here. See MN 2:8.8 and note there.

root_text.sn12.18:1.2: Atha kho timbaruko paribbājako yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavatā saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn12.18:1.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho timbaruko paribbājako bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.18:2.1: “Kiṁ nu kho, bho gotama, sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhanti?
root_text.sn12.18:2.3: Kiṁ pana, bho gotama, paraṅkataṁ sukhadukkhanti?
root_text.sn12.18:2.5: Kiṁ nu kho, bho gotama, sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca sukhadukkhanti?
root_text.sn12.18:2.7: Kiṁ pana, bho gotama, asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ sukhadukkhanti?
root_text.sn12.18:2.9: Kiṁ nu kho, bho gotama, natthi sukhadukkhanti?
root_text.sn12.18:2.12: Tena hi bhavaṁ gotamo sukhadukkhaṁ na jānāti, na passatīti?
root_text.sn12.18:2.13: Na khvāhaṁ, timbaruka, sukhadukkhaṁ na jānāmi, na passāmi.
root_text.sn12.18:3.1: “‘Kiṁ nu kho, bho gotama, sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhan’ti iti puṭṭho samāno ‘mā hevaṁ, timbarukā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn12.18:3.2: ‘Kiṁ pana, bho gotama, paraṅkataṁ sukhadukkhan’ti iti puṭṭho samāno ‘mā hevaṁ, timbarukā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn12.18:3.3: ‘Kiṁ nu kho, bho gotama, sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca sukhadukkhan’ti iti puṭṭho samāno ‘mā hevaṁ, timbarukā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn12.18:3.4: ‘Kiṁ pana, bho gotama, asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ sukhadukkhan’ti iti puṭṭho samāno ‘mā hevaṁ, timbarukā’ti vadesi.
root_text.sn12.18:3.5: ‘Kiṁ nu kho, bho gotama, natthi sukhadukkhan’ti iti puṭṭho samāno ‘na kho, timbaruka, natthi sukhadukkhaṁ;
root_text.sn12.18:3.7: ‘Tena hi bhavaṁ gotamo sukhadukkhaṁ na jānāti, na passatī’ti iti puṭṭho samāno ‘na khvāhaṁ, timbaruka, sukhadukkhaṁ na jānāmi, na passāmi.
root_text.sn12.18:3.10: Ācikkhatu ca me bhavaṁ gotamo sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.18:3.11: Desetu ca me bhavaṁ gotamo sukhadukkhan”ti.
root_text.sn12.18:4.1: “‘Sā vedanā, so vedayatī’ti kho, timbaruka, ādito sato ‘sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhan’ti evampāhaṁ na vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.18:4.2: ‘Aññā vedanā, añño vedayatī’ti kho, timbaruka, vedanābhitunnassa sato ‘paraṅkataṁ sukhadukkhan’ti evampāhaṁ na vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.18:4.3: Ete te, timbaruka, ubho ante anupagamma majjhena tathāgato dhammaṁ deseti:
root_text.sn12.18:5.1: Evaṁ vutte, timbaruko paribbājako bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.18:5.3: esāhaṁ bhavantaṁ gotamaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāmi dhammañca bhikkhusaṅghañca.
root_text.sn12.18:5.4: Upāsakaṁ maṁ bhavaṁ gotamo dhāretu ajjatagge pāṇupetaṁ saraṇaṁ gatan”ti.

translation_text.sn12.18:1.2: Then the wanderer Timbaruka went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn12.18:1.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha:
translation_text.sn12.18:2.1: “Well, worthy Gotama, are pleasure and pain made by oneself?”
translation_text.sn12.18:2.3: “Then are pleasure and pain made by another?”
translation_text.sn12.18:2.5: “Well, are pleasure and pain made by both oneself and another?”
translation_text.sn12.18:2.7: “Then do pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another?”
translation_text.sn12.18:2.9: “Well, is there no such thing as pleasure and pain?”
translation_text.sn12.18:2.10: “It’s not that there’s no such thing as pleasure and pain.
translation_text.sn12.18:2.12: “Then does the worthy Gotama not know or see suffering?”
translation_text.sn12.18:2.13: “It’s not that I don’t know or see pleasure and pain.
translation_text.sn12.18:2.14: I do know pleasure and pain,
translation_text.sn12.18:2.15: I do see pleasure and pain.”
translation_text.sn12.18:3.1: “Worthy Gotama, when asked these questions, you say ‘not so’.
translation_text.sn12.18:3.5: Yet you say that there is such a thing as pleasure and pain.
translation_text.sn12.18:3.8: And you say that you do know pleasure and pain,
translation_text.sn12.18:3.9: and you do see pleasure and pain.
translation_text.sn12.18:3.10: Sir, explain pleasure and pain to me!
translation_text.sn12.18:3.11: Teach me about pleasure and pain!”
translation_text.sn12.18:4.1: “Suppose that the feeling and the one who feels it are one and the same. Then for one who has existed since the beginning, pleasure and pain is made by oneself. I don’t say this.
translation_text.sn12.18:4.2: Suppose that the feeling is one thing and the one who feels it is another. Then for one stricken by feeling, pleasure and pain is made by another. I don’t say this.
translation_text.sn12.18:4.3: Avoiding these two extremes, the Realized One teaches by the middle way:
translation_text.sn12.18:4.5: Choices are a requirement for consciousness. …
translation_text.sn12.18:4.6: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
translation_text.sn12.18:4.7: When ignorance fades away and ceases with nothing left over, choices cease.
translation_text.sn12.18:4.9: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.’”
translation_text.sn12.18:5.1: When he said this, the wanderer Timbaruka said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn12.18:5.3: I go for refuge to the worthy Gotama, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.
translation_text.sn12.18:5.4: From this day forth, may the worthy Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.”
